    demo¶

    This notebook demonstrates how you can use stac-cog-xarray to lazily load massive arrays of Level 2 data from STAC metadata with cloud-optimized geotiff assets.

    In [12]:
    Copied! 
    from pathlib import Path

import rustac
from pyproj import Transformer

import stac_cog_xarray
    from pathlib import Path import rustac from pyproj import Transformer import stac_cog_xarray

    Define the area of interest with a CRS and a bounding box.

    In [13]:
    Copied! 
    # define the AOI in/ a projection that is suitable for your analysis
dst_crs = "epsg:5070"
dst_bbox = (-150_000, 2_500_000, 600_000, 3_000_000)

# transform to epsg:4326 for STAC search
transformer = Transformer.from_crs(dst_crs, "epsg:4326", always_xy=True)
bbox_4326 = transformer.transform_bounds(*dst_bbox)
    # define the AOI in/ a projection that is suitable for your analysis dst_crs = "epsg:5070" dst_bbox = (-150_000, 2_500_000, 600_000, 3_000_000) # transform to epsg:4326 for STAC search transformer = Transformer.from_crs(dst_crs, "epsg:4326", always_xy=True) bbox_4326 = transformer.transform_bounds(*dst_bbox)

    Query a STAC API and cache the result to a local stac-geoparquet file using rustac. This file will be used by the stac-cog-xarray backend to determine which COGs are required for which array operations.

    This example generates an archive of Sentinel 2 Level 2A items for the month of June 2025 for an area that covers a large part of Minnesota, USA.

    In [14]:
    Copied! 
    items_parquet = "/tmp/items.parquet"

if not Path(items_parquet).exists():
    await rustac.search_to(
        items_parquet,
        href="https://earth-search.aws.element84.com/v1",
        collections=["sentinel-2-c1-l2a"],
        datetime="2025-06-01/2025-06-30",
        bbox=bbox_4326,
        limit=100,
    )
    items_parquet = "/tmp/items.parquet" if not Path(items_parquet).exists(): await rustac.search_to( items_parquet, href="https://earth-search.aws.element84.com/v1", collections=["sentinel-2-c1-l2a"], datetime="2025-06-01/2025-06-30", bbox=bbox_4326, limit=100, )

    Open a 100% lazy DataArray representation of the assets referenced in the stac-geoparquet file for your specified CRS and bounding box and resolution.

    In [15]:
    Copied! 
    da = await stac_cog_xarray.open_async(
    items_parquet,
    crs=dst_crs,
    bbox=dst_bbox,
    resolution=100,
    time_period="P1D",
    bands=["red", "green", "blue"],
    dtype="int16",
)

da
    da = await stac_cog_xarray.open_async( items_parquet, crs=dst_crs, bbox=dst_bbox, resolution=100, time_period="P1D", bands=["red", "green", "blue"], dtype="int16", ) da

    Get all of the values for a single point. Add chunk(time=1) to parallelize across time coordinates.

    In [16]:
    Copied! 
    %%time
da.chunk(time=1).sel(x=299965, y=2653947, method="nearest").compute()
    %%time da.chunk(time=1).sel(x=299965, y=2653947, method="nearest").compute()
    CPU times: user 56.6 s, sys: 4.66 s, total: 1min 1s
Wall time: 11.5 s

    Select a spatial subset

    In [17]:
    Copied! 
    subset = da.sel(
    x=slice(100_000, 400_000),
    y=slice(2_600_000, 2_800_000),
)
subset
    subset = da.sel( x=slice(100_000, 400_000), y=slice(2_600_000, 2_800_000), ) subset

    Extract the values for a single point

    In [18]:
    Copied! 
    %%time
subset.isel(time=1, x=0, y=0).compute()
    %%time subset.isel(time=1, x=0, y=0).compute()
    CPU times: user 2.08 s, sys: 205 ms, total: 2.28 s
Wall time: 3.58 s

    Load the spatial subset array for a few time coordinates and plot it.

    In [19]:
    Copied! 
    %%time
subset.isel(time=slice(1, 4)).plot.imshow(rgb="band", row="time", vmin=0, vmax=4000)
    %%time subset.isel(time=slice(1, 4)).plot.imshow(rgb="band", row="time", vmin=0, vmax=4000)
    CPU times: user 1min 54s, sys: 3.94 s, total: 1min 58s
Wall time: 27.3 s
    Out[19]:
    <xarray.plot.facetgrid.FacetGrid at 0x7f80b4d56350>
